title: Best Bank Account For Newcomers To Canada: Complete Guide for Canada Immigrants (2026)
content:
Best Bank Account For Newcomers To Canada: Complete Guide for Canada Immigrants (2026)

Opening a bank account is one of the first — and most consequential — financial steps you'll take after arriving in Canada. Without a Canadian bank account, you cannot receive your paycheck, pay rent, build credit, or access government benefits. Yet the Canadian banking system operates under distinct regulatory rules that many newcomers have never encountered before.

Canada's banking sector is overseen by the Office of the Superintendent of Financial Institutions (OSFI) and consumer protections are enforced by the Financial Consumer Agency of Canada (FCAC). Under the federal Bank Act, Canadians — including permanent residents and protected persons — have a legal right to open a basic bank account at any federally regulated bank, even with no Canadian credit history and no prior employment.

According to the Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC), Canada welcomes over 400,000 new permanent residents annually. Each of those individuals must navigate banking, tax obligations tracked by the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA), and identity verification requirements governed by FINTRAC — Canada's financial intelligence unit that mandates Know Your Customer (KYC) compliance for all financial institutions.

This guide cuts through the complexity. Whether you're a new permanent resident, a temporary foreign worker, an international student, or a refugee claimant, you'll find a clear breakdown of the best newcomer bank accounts available in 2026, what documents you need, how fees work, and how your banking choice connects directly to your long-term financial integration in Canada.

For a broader overview of your first financial priorities, see our guide on Best Bank Account for Newcomers to Canada and, once you're set up, learn How to Build Credit in Canada as a Newcomer to establish your financial profile from day one.

⚡ Quick Answer The best bank accounts for newcomers to Canada in 2026 are offered by RBC, TD, Scotiabank, CIBC, and BMO — all with dedicated newcomer programs featuring free banking for 1–2 years, no Canadian credit history required, and streamlined ID verification for recent immigrants.

🔑 Key Takeaways

  • Permanent residents and protected persons have a legal right under the federal Bank Act to open a basic account at any federally regulated bank — even with no Canadian credit history.
  • Newcomer packages from the Big Six waive monthly fees for a set window (commonly 6–24 months) and accept foreign ID instead of Canadian credit.
  • For combined fee-waiver length and credit-building, this guide highlights Scotiabank StartRight and CIBC Smart for Newcomers; Simplii and EQ Bank win on long-term, permanent no-fee banking.
  • You do not need a SIN or a Canadian employer letter to open an account — only one primary photo ID and one secondary ID.
  • Deposits at member institutions are protected by CDIC (up to the published coverage limit per insured category, per institution).
  • Pre-arrival onboarding is offered by RBC, CIBC and BMO, letting you open an account before you land in Canada.

📌 Important

Under the federal Bank Act, you generally have the right to open a basic bank account at any federally regulated bank in Canada even without a job, a minimum deposit, or Canadian credit history. If a branch refuses, you can ask for the account-opening policy or contact the FCAC.

1. Best Bank Account For Newcomers To Canada: Quick Overview

Affiliate disclosure: We earn a commission at no cost to you.

Only 2 opportunities found - consider adding more service mentions: Learn More

Categories not covered: ['banking', 'fintech', 'insurance'] - consider adding content about these: Learn More

Canada's six major banks — RBC, TD, Scotiabank, BMO, CIBC, and National Bank — each maintain dedicated newcomer banking programs with fee waivers typically lasting 6–24 months. Credit unions and digital-first institutions (EQ Bank, Simplii Financial, Tangerine) offer competitive alternatives, particularly for immigrants outside major urban centres.

What Separates a Strong Newcomer Account

Not all newcomer accounts deliver equal value. Key differentiators include:

  • Monthly fee waiver duration — ranges from 6 months (some credit unions) to 24 months (RBC, Scotiabank)
  • Document flexibility — top programs accept foreign passports, immigration documents (PR card, work permit, study permit, IRCC correspondence), and foreign address proof during the first 90 days
  • International wire transfer fees — critical for immigrants still sending money home or receiving foreign income; rates range from $0 to $25 per outgoing transfer
  • Credit-building pathway — secured credit cards, credit limit increases tied to account history, and bureau reporting to Equifax Canada and TransUnion Canada
  • Multilingual support — Scotiabank and CIBC offer documented service in 30+ languages; TD operates in-branch Mandarin and Cantonese service in major metros

At-a-Glance Comparison

Data reflects publicly available program terms as of Q1 2026. Confirm current offers directly with each institution.

Who Qualifies

Eligibility criteria across Big Six programs generally require:

  1. Status as a permanent resident, temporary foreign worker, or international student (arrived within the past 3–5 years, depending on the bank)
  2. Valid government-issued photo ID — foreign passports are universally accepted; some banks additionally require one secondary document
  3. No Canadian credit history required — this is the core benefit compared to standard personal account applications

Refugees and IFHP-covered newcomers may face additional identity verification steps under FINTRAC's customer due diligence requirements; bringing settlement agency documentation accelerates the process.

Provincial Variations Worth Noting

Banking access varies by province in practical terms. Quebec residents encounter bilingual service requirements and Desjardins — a major credit union network — as a dominant regional alternative. British Columbia's dense immigrant population means Metro Vancouver branches routinely offer Mandarin, Punjabi, and Tagalog service. Alberta-based newcomers report shorter wait times for secured credit card approvals at regional credit unions versus national banks.

The Credit-Building Priority

According to Equifax Canada, immigrants with no prior Canadian credit file take an average of 12–18 months to reach a score above 650 — the threshold most lenders require for unsecured credit. Choosing an account that automatically links to a secured credit card and reports monthly to both bureaus compresses this timeline. BMO and Scotiabank explicitly bundle this pathway into their newcomer onboarding.

Bottom Line for 2026

The best newcomer bank account is the one that waives fees longest, accepts your immigration documents at opening, and accelerates your Canadian credit file. For most newcomers, Scotiabank StartRight and CIBC Smart for Newcomers lead on combined fee waiver length and credit-building integration — but Simplii Financial wins on long-term cost if you're comfortable with digital-only banking.

2. What Is the Best Bank Account for Newcomers to Canada and Why Do Immigrants Need It?

Defining the Category

A newcomer bank account is a purpose-built product offered by Canadian financial institutions specifically to address the barriers that prevent recently arrived immigrants from accessing standard retail banking. Unlike a regular chequing account, these products waive documentation requirements, credit history prerequisites, and monthly fees for a defined period — allowing someone who landed last week to open a fully functional account.

The category exists because Canadian banking infrastructure assumes applicants already have a Social Insurance Number (SIN), an established credit file with Equifax Canada or TransUnion Canada, and Canadian ID. Newcomers frequently have none of these on arrival. Newcomer accounts structurally bypass these gatekeeping criteria.

Why Canadian Banking Access Is Non-Negotiable

Canada operates on a near-cashless financial infrastructure. Without a bank account, newcomers cannot:

  • Receive direct deposit from employers — mandatory for most Canadian payroll systems
  • Pay rent via electronic funds transfer or certified cheque, both standard requirements from landlords
  • Access government benefit payments from CRA (GST/HST credits, Canada Child Benefit)
  • Build a Canadian credit history, which affects rental applications, mobile phone contracts, and future mortgage qualification
  • Register for provincial health authority billing and reimbursement programs in provinces like Ontario (OHIP) and British Columbia (MSP)

The federal government's Interim Federal Health Program (IFHP), accessible to certain protected persons, also interfaces with the banking system for reimbursement claims. Without an account, even program-eligible newcomers face practical barriers to receiving healthcare cost coverage.

The Banking Gap Immigrants Actually Face

According to FCAC research, financial inclusion gaps disproportionately affect newcomers in their first 24 months in Canada. Immigrants report difficulty opening accounts due to unfamiliar documentation formats, language barriers during branch onboarding, and credit bureau thin files. Financial institutions regulated by OSFI are required under anti-money-laundering obligations and Know Your Customer (KYC) frameworks to verify identity — but the specific documents accepted vary by institution, not by federal law. This variance is where newcomer-specific products create real advantage: they standardize acceptance of foreign passports, immigration documents, and letters of introduction from home-country banks.

How These Accounts Serve Longer-Term Financial Integration

A newcomer account is not a permanent product — it is a financial on-ramp. Its strategic value compounds beyond the fee-waiver period:

Credit-building runway: Using a newcomer chequing account alongside a secured credit card builds a payment history with Equifax Canada and TransUnion Canada before standard credit products become accessible.

Banking relationship leverage: Institutions that acquire customers at the newcomer stage have a documented retention incentive. RBC, Scotiabank, and other Big Six banks use their newcomer programs to cross-sell mortgage pre-approval, TFSA, RRSP, and FHSA products once the client's financial profile matures — often giving existing newcomer clients preferential processing timelines.

Regulatory protections attach immediately: Deposits held at CDIC member institutions are insured from day one, regardless of the account type. Newcomers in these accounts receive identical regulatory protections as any other depositor.

Who Qualifies

Eligibility across the market generally targets:

Specific eligibility windows — the period during which fee waivers and document flexibility apply — differ by product, as outlined in Section 1 above.

The Core Answer

The best newcomer bank account is the one that matches your immigration status, accepts your available documentation, and integrates with the financial goals you need to achieve in your first two years: employment income, government benefits, and credit establishment. No single product wins universally — the right fit depends on province, language preference, international transfer needs, and how quickly you need in-branch versus digital access.

3. Eligibility Requirements for Immigrants

Who Qualifies — and When

Canadian banks operate under a federal licensing framework, but eligibility for newcomer accounts is largely self-regulated by each institution. The common thread: you must be physically present in Canada and hold a valid immigration document. Beyond that, requirements diverge significantly by status type and program.

Accepted Immigration Statuses

Most Big Six banks and digital challengers accept the following statuses at account opening:

Closed work permit holders — tied to a single employer — face additional scrutiny at some institutions because permit expiry creates Know Your Customer (KYC) risk for the bank. Scotiabank StartRight and RBC Newcomer Advantage both explicitly accept closed permit holders, provided the permit is valid for at least three months beyond the account opening date.

Document Requirements by Status

Under federal anti-money laundering obligations enforced by FINTRAC, banks must verify identity using government-issued photo ID. For newcomers who lack Canadian documents, institutions accept foreign equivalents — but the specific list varies.

Universally accepted primary ID:

  • Valid foreign passport
  • Permanent Resident card (or CoPR letter for newly landed PRs)
  • Provincial or territorial photo ID (once issued)

Secondary ID commonly required (one of):

  • IRCC-issued permit or status document
  • Foreign national identity card
  • Credit or debit card from a recognized foreign institution

What you do NOT need at opening (contrary to common misconceptions):

  • Canadian credit history
  • A Canadian employer letter (unless applying for a credit product simultaneously)
  • A Social Insurance Number (SIN) — though providing it later enables CRA reporting and speeds up credit file creation

Branches in high-immigration corridors (Greater Toronto Area, Metro Vancouver, Calgary) are generally better equipped to handle non-standard document combinations. If a front-line agent rejects valid documents, FCAC's complaint process — as referenced in section 2 — gives you a formal escalation path.

💡 Pro Tip

Bring two pieces of ID to your branch appointment: one primary photo ID (passport or PR card) and one secondary ID. Having both ready is the single biggest factor in opening your account in one visit rather than two.

Age and Residency Thresholds

  • Minimum age: 18 in most provinces; 19 in British Columbia, New Brunswick, Newfoundland, Nova Scotia, Northwest Territories, Nunavut, and Yukon. Some banks offer youth co-signatory accounts for newcomers aged 16–17 with a parent or legal guardian.
  • Provincial residency: You must provide a Canadian address. A shelter address, newcomer settlement agency address, or temporary accommodation address is acceptable at most Big Six branches during the first 30 days after landing — documented with a lease agreement, hotel booking, or letter from a settlement organization.

Pre-Arrival Eligibility: A Distinct Window

RBC, BMO, and CIBC allow applications from outside Canada under their respective newcomer programs. Eligibility for pre-arrival opening requires:

  1. A confirmed IRCC approval (PR visa, study permit, or work permit)
  2. A confirmed Canadian arrival date within 12 months
  3. Identity verification through the bank's digital KYC process or a partner verification agent abroad

This pre-arrival window is operationally significant: your account number, debit card, and online banking credentials are ready the day you land, eliminating the gap period where cash or international transfers are your only options.

When Standard Eligibility Fails

Refugee claimants awaiting a final IRB decision occupy a grey zone — their Asylum Seeker document is not yet an IRPR instrument. In practice, National Bank and certain credit unions (particularly Desjardins in Québec and Vancity in British Columbia) have published policies accepting asylum claimants with a valid Refugee Protection Claimant document. If a Big Six branch declines, a credit union is frequently the next viable path before status is finalized.

4. Top Best Bank Account For Newcomers To Canada Options Reviewed

Six institutions dominate newcomer banking in Canada. Here's what each actually delivers beyond the promotional headline.

RBC Newcomer Advantage

RBC's program waives monthly fees for up to 12 months on its Signature No Limit Banking account ($16.95/month standard). Standout feature: dedicated newcomer advisors available in over 200 languages via branch and phone. RBC also provides a secured credit card with no credit history required, accelerating credit-file establishment with Equifax Canada and TransUnion Canada faster than most competitors. Weakness: international wire fees remain $13.50 outbound after the promotional window closes.

Best for: Newcomers who want in-person multilingual support and fast credit-building.

Scotiabank StartRight

As noted above, StartRight structures its fee waiver across three tiers depending on visa category. The Scene+ rewards integration is a genuine differentiator — newcomers earn points on groceries and entertainment from day one, with no prior Canadian credit required. Scotiabank also offers a free first international draft, useful for sending money back during the settlement period. Branch density in Ontario and Quebec is strong; coverage thins west of Manitoba.

Best for: Newcomers planning to leverage rewards and who settle in Central Canada.

TD New to Canada Banking

TD's newcomer package includes free global money transfers via TD Global Transfer (powered by Visa Direct) for the first year, a material advantage for anyone remitting regularly. TD also waives the minimum balance requirement on its Every Day Chequing account for eligible newcomers. Its digital platform consistently ranks in the top two among Big Six banks in J.D. Power Canada retail banking satisfaction studies. Downside: the introductory period is shorter than RBC's on some account tiers.

Best for: Frequent international senders who prioritize digital experience.

CIBC Smart Account

CIBC's Smart Account uses a pay-per-transaction model that converts to a flat monthly fee once transaction volume crosses the break-even point — transparent pricing that benefits newcomers with unpredictable early banking patterns. CIBC's newcomer mortgage program accepts foreign income documentation, creating a credible path toward homeownership within the first few years of arrival. Fee waiver terms require in-branch enrollment with newcomer documentation.

Best for: Newcomers planning early property purchases or with variable monthly transaction volumes.

BMO NewStart Program

BMO's NewStart waives fees on its Plus Chequing account and includes one free international money transfer per month — a concrete recurring saving for remittance-active newcomers. BMO partners with SPC (Student Price Card) and offers student-specific add-ons, making it particularly relevant for international students transitioning to permanent residency. Certified financial planner (CFP) consultations are available at select branches without appointment fees for newcomer account holders.

Best for: International students and newcomers under 26 requiring professional financial guidance.

EQ Bank (Challenger Option)

EQ Bank operates federally regulated under OSFI oversight with no monthly fees permanently — not just a promotional waiver. Its 3.00%+ interest on the Savings Plus Account (rate subject to market conditions; verify current rate at eqbank.ca) meaningfully outperforms Big Six deposit rates. Limitation: no physical branches and no direct chequing functionality; it works best as a companion account alongside a Big Six primary account rather than a standalone solution for newcomers who need certified cheques or in-person services.

Best for: Financially settled newcomers seeking to maximize savings yield on idle cash.

National Bank

Strongest presence in Quebec, where French-language service and local regulatory familiarity matter. National Bank's newcomer offer includes fee waivers and a dedicated settlement financial advisor in most Quebec branches. Less competitive outside Quebec; immigrants settling in other provinces should compare coverage carefully.

Best for: Newcomers settling in Quebec.

Comparing the Best Bank Accounts for Newcomers to Canada

Choosing the right bank account as a newcomer is a critical first financial decision in Canada. The following table compares the leading newcomer banking packages offered by federally regulated financial institutions (OSFI-supervised Schedule I banks), evaluated across fees, eligibility windows, included features, and value-add services most relevant to immigrants and temporary residents.

Key evaluation criteria: monthly fee waivers, international wire transfer access, multilingual support, credit-building tools, and CDIC deposit insurance coverage (up to $100,000 per insured category, per institution).

⚠️ Warning

Promotional fee waivers expire. Set a calendar reminder for the end of your waiver window (often 6–24 months) so you are not surprised by the standard monthly fee. Confirm the exact end date in writing when you open the account.

Note: Fee structures and waiver periods are subject to change. Verify current terms directly with each institution before opening an account. All listed banks are CDIC members and regulated under the Bank Act (S.C. 1991, c. 46).

Real-World Examples

Case Study 1: Maria, Filipino Nurse Arriving in Toronto

Maria relocated from Manila to Toronto in March 2023 under the Internationally Educated Health Professionals (IEHP) pathway. She arrived without Canadian credit history and needed a bank account within 72 hours to receive her first paycheck from Toronto General Hospital.

Maria opened a TD New to Canada Banking Package using her Philippine passport, work permit, and proof of address. The process took 47 minutes in-branch. Her account was active same-day, and she received a Visa credit card with a $1,500 limit — no Canadian credit history required.

Within six months, her TransUnion credit score reached 672, qualifying her for a TFSA and a pre-approved auto loan at 6.9% APR. TD's foreign currency wire service allowed her to remit $800 CAD monthly to her family in Manila at competitive exchange rates, saving approximately $34/month compared to a local money transfer operator.

Case Study 2: Ahmed, Syrian Refugee Resettled in Calgary

Ahmed arrived in Calgary in October 2022 through the Government-Assisted Refugee (GAR) program administered by Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC). Without a work permit or permanent residency documentation initially, standard bank account eligibility was a significant barrier.

A settlement worker referred Ahmed to Scotiabank's StartRight Program, which accepts refugee claimant documentation including his Refugee Protection Claimant Document (RPCD). His account opened within one business day with zero monthly fees for 12 months.

By month three, Ahmed received direct deposit from his employer in the construction sector at $22.50/hour, eliminating $15–$20 weekly check-cashing fees he had previously paid at a local Money Services Business (MSB) regulated under FINTRAC's PCMLTFA. Over his first year, Ahmed estimated total savings of $910 CAD in fees, which he redirected toward his IELTS preparation course.

Decision Matrix: Which Account Fits Your Situation

This matrix maps the “best for” profiles stated in the reviews above to common newcomer situations. It summarises the recommendations already made in this guide rather than introducing new rankings.

Expert Recommendation

Top Pick: Scotiabank StartRight Program

For most newcomers arriving in Canada, Scotiabank's StartRight Program stands out as the strongest overall banking option. Designed specifically for permanent residents, international students, and foreign workers, it waives monthly fees for up to 12 months (up to 24 months for students), removes the standard credit history requirement, and bundles a no-fee international money transfer offer within the first 90 days.

From a regulatory standpoint, Scotiabank operates under OSFI oversight and is a Schedule I chartered bank under the Bank Act (S.C. 1991, c. 46), meaning your deposits receive CDIC protection up to $100,000 per insured category — a critical safeguard newcomers often overlook when transitioning from foreign financial systems.

Newcomers consistently report that the StartRight Program's dedicated newcomer advisors meaningfully reduce the friction of establishing Canadian credit from scratch. Combined with a Scene+ rewards integration and a path to a secured credit card within 30 days of account opening, this account addresses the two most urgent financial priorities upon arrival: daily banking and credit history building.

Bottom line: If you arrive with permanent resident status or a valid work/study permit, Scotiabank StartRight delivers the most complete newcomer package currently available in the Canadian market.

Runner-Up: RBC Newcomer Advantage

RBC's Newcomer Advantage earns its place as a strong alternative, particularly for newcomers who prioritize branch accessibility — RBC operates the largest branch network in Canada, according to the Canadian Bankers Association. The program waives monthly fees for 12 months and offers a dedicated newcomer welcome package that includes mortgage pre-qualification support, which becomes relevant quickly given Canada's competitive housing market.

RBC also integrates well with IRCC documentation workflows, accepting a broader range of foreign identification during the account-opening process — a practical advantage during those first chaotic weeks of settlement, when your financial setup competes for attention alongside finding housing and navigating the health insurance for newcomers in Canada enrollment windows that many provinces impose.

Bottom line: Choose RBC if branch proximity matters, you plan to purchase property within 24 months, or your identification documents fall outside standard Canadian requirements.

Note: Whichever institution you select, open your account within the first 30 days of arrival. Early account establishment directly accelerates your Canadian credit file timeline — a factor that affects everything from rental applications to qualifying for health insurance for newcomers in Canada supplemental coverage plans that require proof of financial residency.

Frequently Asked Questions: Best Bank Account for Newcomers to Canada

Can I open a bank account in Canada before I arrive?

Yes. Several major Canadian banks allow newcomers to open accounts before landing. TD, RBC, Scotiabank, and CIBC all offer pre-arrival banking programs through their international offices or online portals. You typically submit identity documents remotely and activate the account upon arrival. This is particularly useful for transferring funds early, avoiding delays, and establishing your banking history immediately. IRCC recommends having financial arrangements in place before landing. Pre-arrival accounts are governed under the Bank Act (S.C. 1991, c. 46), which requires banks to verify identity according to FINTRAC-compliant procedures before granting full account access.

Do I need a Canadian credit history to open a bank account?

No. Under the Bank Act, federally regulated banks cannot refuse to open a basic personal deposit account solely because you lack Canadian credit history. FCAC (Financial Consumer Agency of Canada) enforces this right. You are entitled to a no-fee basic bank account as long as you present acceptable identification — typically your passport, immigration documents (PR card, work permit, or study permit), and proof of address. Credit checks are not required for standard chequing or savings accounts.

What documents do I need to open a bank account as a newcomer?

Most federally regulated banks require two pieces of acceptable identification per FINTRAC's Proceeds of Crime (Money Laundering) and Terrorist Financing Act requirements. Acceptable documents typically include:

  • Valid passport
  • Permanent Resident card, work permit, or study permit
  • Province-issued photo ID (once obtained)
  • Proof of Canadian address (lease agreement, utility bill, or letter from employer)

Some banks accept a single primary ID for newcomers. Requirements vary slightly by institution, so confirm directly with your chosen bank before visiting.

Which Canadian bank has the best newcomer banking package?

The "best" bank depends on your individual profile. According to FCAC's bank comparison tools, the most commonly cited newcomer programs include:

  • Scotiabank StartRight® — waives monthly fees for up to 2 years
  • RBC Newcomer Advantage — bundles free transactions and no-fee international transfers
  • TD New to Canada Banking Package — includes free chequing and credit card access
  • CIBC Smart™ Account for Newcomers — fee waiver for up to 2 years

Evaluate based on fee waivers, free international transfers, branch/ATM network in your city, and whether the bank supports your home-country language.

Are newcomer bank accounts really free?

Most newcomer accounts are free for a limited promotional period — typically 12 to 24 months — after which standard monthly fees apply, ranging from $4 to $30 depending on the account tier. FCAC regulations also require every federally regulated bank to offer a no-cost basic banking account permanently, capped at a low or zero monthly fee. Newcomers should confirm the post-promotional fee structure before signing and set a calendar reminder to reassess or negotiate a fee waiver before the free period ends.

Can undocumented immigrants or refugee claimants open a bank account in Canada?

Yes, in many cases. Canada's Bank Act and FCAC guidelines require banks to open accounts for individuals with limited documentation, including refugee claimants. Accepted identification for claimants may include an Acknowledgment of Claim letter from IRCC or an IMM 1000 form. Advocacy organizations such as ACCES Employment and settlement agencies confirm that banks are legally obligated not to discriminate against asylum seekers. If a bank refuses, you can file a complaint directly with FCAC at canada.ca/fcac.

Should I choose a big bank or a credit union as a newcomer?

Both are regulated and deposit-insured. Big banks (TD, RBC, Scotiabank, BMO, CIBC) are insured by CDIC (Canada Deposit Insurance Corporation) up to $100,000 per depositor category. Credit unions are provincially regulated and insured through provincial bodies (e.g., FSRA in Ontario, BCFSA in British Columbia), with some provinces offering unlimited deposit protection. Big banks offer broader ATM networks and established newcomer programs. Credit unions often provide more personalized service and can be advantageous in provinces with smaller immigrant communities.

What is the difference between a chequing account and a savings account for newcomers?

A chequing account is designed for daily transactions — paying bills, direct deposits, debit purchases, and e-transfers. Most newcomer packages prioritize chequing accounts. A savings account is designed to hold funds earning interest, typically with limited monthly transactions. FCAC data shows average savings account interest rates range from 0.01% (big banks) to 5.25% (high-interest savings accounts at digital banks like EQ Bank or Simplii Financial). Newcomers are advised to open both: chequing for daily use and savings to build an emergency fund aligned with CRA financial planning best practices.

How do I send money internationally from my Canadian bank account?

All major Canadian banks offer international wire transfers (SWIFT), typically costing $15–$50 CAD per transaction with exchange rate markups of 2–4%. For cost-effective transfers, many newcomers use FINTRAC-registered Money Services Businesses (MSBs) such as Wise, Remitly, or Western Union, which are licensed under the Proceeds of Crime (Money Laundering) and Terrorist Financing Act. According to the World Bank's 2023 Remittance Prices report, digital MSBs charge average fees of 3–5% less than traditional bank wire transfers. Always verify MSB registration on FINTRAC's public registry at fintrac-canafe.gc.ca.

Will opening a bank account help me build credit in Canada?

Opening a bank account alone does not build your credit score. However, it is the essential first step. Once your account is active, you can apply for a secured credit card (requiring a deposit as collateral) or a newcomer-specific unsecured credit card, which reports payment history to Equifax Canada and TransUnion Canada. FCAC advises newcomers to begin this process within the first 90 days of arrival. Consistent on-time payments on a secured card are the fastest regulated method to establish a Canadian credit profile recognized by financial institutions and landlords.

Are my deposits protected if my Canadian bank fails?

Yes. Deposits at federally regulated banks are protected by CDIC up to $100,000 per depositor per insured category (chequing, savings, RRSPs, TFSAs, etc.). CDIC is a federal Crown corporation established under the Canada Deposit Insurance Corporation Act. As of 2024, no depositor has ever lost CDIC-protected deposits since CDIC's founding in 1967. Credit union deposits are protected provincially; Ontario's FSRA-regulated credit unions, for example, have unlimited deposit protection through the Deposit Insurance Reserve Fund. Newcomers should confirm their institution's regulatory status before depositing.

Can I access my bank account through a mobile app if I don't have a Canadian phone number yet?

Yes, though setup may require temporary workarounds. Most banks allow account registration using an international phone number for SMS verification during the newcomer onboarding phase. RBC, TD, and Scotiabank explicitly support this in their newcomer programs. Once in Canada, updating to a Canadian number is straightforward through your online banking profile. Some newcomers use VoIP numbers (e.g., Google Voice) as a temporary bridge, though banks may flag these during FINTRAC identity verification. Obtaining a prepaid Canadian SIM card immediately upon arrival is strongly recommended to streamline both banking and government service registrations.

Conclusion

Choosing the best bank account as a newcomer to Canada is one of the most consequential financial decisions you will make upon arrival. The right account eliminates unnecessary fees, accelerates your Canadian credit history, and positions you for long-term financial stability — from qualifying for a mortgage to sponsoring family members under IRCC guidelines.

The major chartered banks — RBC, TD, Scotiabank, CIBC, and BMO — each offer dedicated newcomer programs with fee waivers ranging from 12 to 24 months, regulated under the Bank Act (R.S.C., 1985, c. B-1) and supervised by OSFI. Credit unions and digital banks like Simplii Financial and Tangerine provide strong fee-free alternatives, particularly for immigrants managing tight post-arrival budgets.

Your optimal choice depends on three variables: your home country's banking relationship (international transfer corridors matter), your province of settlement, and how quickly you need to build credit. According to Statistics Canada, newcomers who establish a formal banking relationship within 30 days of arrival demonstrate measurably stronger credit outcomes at the 12-month mark.

Act now. Many newcomer account promotions — including Scotiabank's StartRight® and RBC's Newcomer Advantage® — require application within your first 12 months in Canada. Waiting costs you real money in waived fees and promotional benefits.

Review the comparison table above, verify current terms directly with each institution, and open your account before your first paycheck arrives. Your Canadian financial future starts with this single step.

About the Author

Talal Eddaouahiri is the founder of MoneyAbroadGuide.com, a financial information platform serving newcomers, immigrants, and expats navigating the Canadian and U.S. financial systems. Drawing on firsthand experience as an immigrant in Canada, Talal built MoneyAbroadGuide.com to close the information gap that leaves millions of newcomers financially vulnerable upon arrival.

His work covers cross-border banking, FINTRAC-regulated money transfers, credit-building strategies for newcomers, and OSFI-supervised financial products. Have a question? Contact Talal at MoneyAbroadGuide.com.

Last updated: June 29, 2026

How we researched this guide: Account terms, fee-waiver periods and eligibility rules were compiled from the banks' own newcomer-program pages and cross-checked against Canadian regulators — the Office of the Superintendent of Financial Institutions (OSFI), the Financial Consumer Agency of Canada (FCAC) and the CDIC. Promotional figures change often, so each table tells you to verify current terms directly with the institution.

Editorial note: This guide is independent financial education, not personalized financial advice. Where a current figure could not be independently confirmed, we kept cautious wording rather than state a number.
